text:
That is lovely!

I found it funny that "no if/else or switch" was immediately followed by an example using the keyword `if`. And it got me thinking: Have you considered the keyword `where`? Seems to flow better as a sentence:

"`when` entity `is` out_of_range `if` distance self out_of_range > 1 `then` ..."

as opposed to "`when` entity `is` out_of_range `where` distance self out_of_range > 1 `then` ..."

— the first one grammatically to me seems to invite one-to-many branches "when entity is out_of_range [then] if ... otherwise ..." rather than one-to-one "when entity is out_of_range (where [defining out_of_range]) then ...".

I guess the latter could also invite syntax flexibility, having the where clauses next to their targets or gathering them at the end of the "when" statement, it was a long time since I did anything in Haskell but that seems like an idea I must have gotten from there right?

I wouldn't have mentioned it, but you referred to toki pona so discussing choice of words didn't seem too far off topic :^) Also, when skimming the rest I see you are using `where` in the array programming interface (which I guess is in userland rather than a keyword but alas), so thematically you already have a `where` playing this role, which should make it enticing to simplify both cases to the same word, right...? :revolving_hearts:

/Ivan

text:
Maybe this is not relevant, but your initial assumption "we know that recursion consists of 2 parts" made me think of a great video by Nic Barker called <https://youtu.be/YuaJ8x_NcLw?si=ORSqExEx46ii2Sot|Recursion, Explained Simply> - where he in 1 hour goes through the different edge cases and peculiarities of recursion that make it more complicated than what meets the eye.

It made me remember why recursion (and loops in general) really can trip up beginners.

On topic I agree that academic language can be uninviting. But I think that its use is fueled by how it can also be surprisingly internally consistent. Having Latin based naming for anatomical parts, orientations, crossings, etc. in medicine allow us to communicate across diciplines and spoken languages. We even have semi-standardization for other terms by the use of a constant number of keywords used in specific databases and services to search for and translate those keywords (MESH for PubMed have given rise to "Svensk MESH", a service where we can search in Swedish and explore the overlapping hierarchies of the PubMed keywords). And I think that other areas assume that this success story translates to all diciplines. Missing that the historic body of work is not purely academic (actually not even in medicine, but that is <https://youtu.be/eQdTmvqCgxI?si=pPQ_n5xJyQNDczIb|another tangent>), and missing that a lot of what makes someting human-readable is whether it is human-learnable and human-teachable. And with that it is easy to see how community, education (and therefore also: capital) plays a big role.
